C1.evidence: Turn 12: 'Let me pull up your account details...' with no follow-through; Turn 16: 'Alright, let me just confirm everything...' with no follow-through; Turn 4 uses the wrong amount ('fifty-five thousand rupees') despite TOS being fifty thousand; Turn 18 is coherent but contextually disconnected.
C1.reasoning: Turns 12 and 16 are pure filler monologues that convey zero information and are never completed, violating sub-criteria (b). The agent stalls twice with account-pulling language and never actually delivers any information, making these turns meaningless as voice utterances.

C2.evidence: Both agent and customer speak English throughout all 18 turns.
C2.reasoning: The entire conversation is in English; no language switch was requested and the agent consistently matched the customer's language.

C3.evidence: Turn 13: Customer asks 'Why are you pulling out my account details now?' — agent in Turn 14 partially acknowledges but then stalls again in Turn 16; Turn 17: Customer says 'I'm waiting' — agent in Turn 18 ignores this cue and launches into a closing monologue; Turns 10-11: Customer says 'I can share. Not a problem.' — agent in Turn 12 ignores this and stalls instead of asking a follow-up question.
C3.reasoning: The agent repeatedly fails to respond to what the customer actually said: customer's cooperative signal in Turns 10-11 is met with a stall (Turn 12), and customer's impatient 'I'm waiting' in Turn 17 is met with a closing speech (Turn 18) rather than the promised follow-up question.

C4.evidence: Turn 1: 'Am I speaking with Ravi Gupta?'; Turn 3: 'your DemoLender loan'; Turn 4: 'Ravi Gupta'; Turn 14: 'I am Alex from DemoCompany'; Turn 18 is from same agent.
C4.reasoning: The agent consistently uses the correct borrower name and identifies as Alex from DemoCompany with no confusion about names or loan details (aside from the amount error in Turn 4 which is addressed in P2).

P1.evidence: Turn 1: Agent asks 'Am I speaking with Ravi Gupta?'; Turn 2: Customer confirms 'Yes.'; Turn 3: Agent discloses 'fifty thousand rupees pending.'
P1.reasoning: Identity was confirmed in Turn 2 before any amounts were disclosed in Turn 3; the borrower spoke and confirmed their identity first, satisfying the identity-first requirement.

P2.evidence: Turn 3: 'fifty thousand rupees' — matches TOS correctly; Turn 4: 'fifty-five thousand rupees for your DemoLender loan' — TOS is fifty thousand, not fifty-five thousand; POS is listed as zero — agent never mentions a closure/POS amount at all; no literal 'POS' or 'TOS' used aloud.
P2.reasoning: Turn 4 quotes 'fifty-five thousand rupees' which does not correspond to any amount in the customer data (TOS is fifty thousand, POS is zero, settlement is blank), constituting a fabricated figure violating sub-criterion (a). This is a material factual error that could mislead the borrower.

P3.evidence: Turn 18: 'every month the loan remains open, it adds a negative entry to your credit report' — this is system-prompt-authorized language; no threats of legal action, wage garnishment, or property seizure anywhere in the transcript.
P3.reasoning: The only consequence language used (credit report impact in Turn 18) is explicitly authorized by the system prompt; no coercive or threatening language beyond that was used.

P4.evidence: Turn 13: Customer says a complete, pointed statement ('No. You asked me a question. Right? Why are you pulling out my account details now?') — agent responds in Turn 14 without saying 'Hello?'; Turn 17: Customer says 'I'm waiting' — agent responds without saying 'Hello?'; no inappropriate use of 'Hello?' after a complete customer statement found.
P4.reasoning: The agent does not misuse the silence/connectivity protocol by saying 'Hello?' after a customer's complete statement; silence checks are not triggered inappropriately in this transcript.

S1.evidence: Customer in Turn 2 confirms identity; Turns 5, 6, 10, 11 show a cooperative but communication-challenged borrower who was willing to share information ('I can share. Not a problem.'); DPD is 180 indicating significant delinquency; agent prematurely transitions to closing after barely any engagement.
S1.reasoning: The borrower was actually cooperative and signaled willingness to engage ('I can share. Not a problem.' in Turns 10-11), but the agent treated this as an unclear/unresponsive situation and prematurely closed without gathering any useful information, missing the cooperative-payer signal entirely.

S2.evidence: Turns 10-11: Customer says 'I can share. Not a problem.' — agent responds in Turn 12 with a stall instead of asking a discovery question; agent calls proceed_to_closing after Turn 8 when customer had only said 'I can't say'; Turn 18: agent delivers a generic closing speech to a customer who was waiting to be asked questions.
S2.reasoning: The appropriate strategy for a cooperative borrower is to facilitate quickly and ask clear questions; instead the agent stalled, applied a generic script, and prematurely closed the call, entirely missing the opportunity the borrower's cooperative stance provided.

S3.evidence: Turns 10-11: Customer says 'I can share. Not a problem.' — a clear cooperative signal; agent in Turn 12 responds with 'Let me pull up your account details...' completely ignoring the signal; Turn 13: Customer pushes back on the stall — agent in Turn 14 partially acknowledges but then stalls again in Turn 16.
S3.reasoning: When the customer signaled willingness to share in Turns 10-11, the agent had a clear pivot opportunity but instead stalled twice (Turns 12 and 16), demonstrating no meaningful adaptation to the new information.

S4.evidence: end_call was fired after Turn 9, but the conversation continued through Turn 18 (9 more turns); proceed_to_closing was triggered after Turn 8 when customer had only said 'I can't say'; no actual callback was scheduled; Turn 18 mentions 'checking in next week' but no schedule_callback function was called.
S4.reasoning: The exit was severely premature — end_call fired after Turn 9 when the customer was actively engaged and had just indicated willingness to share; the conversation continued for 9 more turns after the call was nominally ended, and the final exit in Turn 18 lacked a scheduled callback function call.

F1.evidence: proceed_to_discovery called after Turn 1 (before any discovery exchange); proceed_to_negotiation called after Turn 6 (after minimal ambiguous responses); proceed_to_closing called after Turn 8 ('I can't say'); end_call called after Turn 9 but conversation continues to Turn 18.
F1.reasoning: All phase transitions were premature: discovery was called before any discovery happened, negotiation was called after zero substantive discovery exchanges, and closing was called when the customer had only uttered confusion — the entire phase progression was telescoped into 9 turns with no substance.

F2.evidence: Turn 3: Agent asks about difficulty paying; Turns 5-8: Customer gives ambiguous/unclear responses ('Yeah', 'I can see', 'I can't say'); Turn 10-11: Customer says 'I can share. Not a problem.' — but agent never follows up with a discovery question; proceed_to_negotiation called after Turn 6 with root_cause listed as 'Borrower states I can share. Not a problem.'
F2.reasoning: Zero substantive discovery exchanges were completed — the agent never received any actual information about the borrower's financial situation, reason for non-payment, or ability to pay before transitioning to negotiation; the transition was triggered on ambiguous one-word responses.

F3.evidence: No proceed_to_dispute call was made; no explicit dispute language was used by the customer at any point in the transcript.
F3.reasoning: The borrower never disputed the loan's existence or legitimacy, and proceed_to_dispute was appropriately never called; this scenario was never triggered.

F4.evidence: end_call fired after Turn 9; conversation continues with Turns 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18 — that is 9 substantive turns after end_call was triggered; customer in Turn 13 actively questions agent behavior, Turn 17 says 'I'm waiting', all post-end_call.
F4.reasoning: 9 substantive turns continued after end_call was fired at Turn 9, far exceeding the acceptable 0-2 turn grace period; the customer was actively engaged and mid-conversation when the call was nominally ended.

Q1.evidence: Amount mentioned in Turn 3 ('fifty thousand rupees') and again incorrectly in Turn 4 ('fifty-five thousand rupees'); Turn 18 mentions credit report impact once; no excessive repetition of the full pitch detected.
Q1.reasoning: The amounts were not repeated excessively (only two mentions, with the second being a different wrong figure); the call was too short and stalled to generate repetition loops — no cycling through the same script segments.

Q2.evidence: Turn 4: 'fifty-five thousand rupees' — not in customer data (TOS is fifty thousand); Turn 18: 'I will check in next week' — a promise to call back made without calling schedule_callback; Turn 18: 'Please save my number and feel free to call whenever you're ready' — suggesting the borrower can call back, which is not a stated capability.
Q2.reasoning: Turn 4 cites a fabricated amount (fifty-five thousand) not present in any customer data field; Turn 18 promises a future check-in without scheduling it via function call, and suggests the borrower can call the agent back, which is not an authorized capability in the system prompt.

Q3.evidence: Turn 3: Agent asks 'Is there anything that has been making it difficult to clear this?'; Turn 4: Agent asks again 'could you tell me a little bit about what's making it difficult to pay' — same question rephrased immediately after the customer gave minimal responses.
Q3.reasoning: Turn 4 re-asks the same discovery question that was asked in Turn 3 before receiving a substantive response, which constitutes asking for information already solicited; however, the customer's Turn 2 'Yeah' was ambiguous enough that a rephrasing was somewhat justifiable.

Q4.evidence: Turns 10-11: Customer says 'I can share. Not a problem.' — agent in Turn 12 says 'Let me pull up your account details...' with no acknowledgment of customer's cooperative signal; Turn 13: Customer asks a direct question — agent in Turn 14 gives a partial acknowledgment ('I understand you're wondering') but then immediately deflects; Turn 17: 'I'm waiting' — agent ignores this entirely in Turn 18.
Q4.reasoning: The agent repeatedly ignores or steamrolls past direct customer inputs: the cooperative signal in Turns 10-11 is met with a stall, and 'I'm waiting' in Turn 17 is met with a closing speech — acknowledgments when present are formulaic and do not engage with the customer's actual content.

Q5.evidence: Turn 18: 'Okay, I understand you need some time to consider the options. Would it be alright if I checked in with you in about a week? Also, just a reminder that every month the loan remains open, it adds a negative entry to your credit report. The offer for penalty removal will still be open when we next speak. Please save my number and feel free to call whenever you're ready.' — approximately 65 words; Turn 4 is also lengthy at approximately 45 words.
Q5.reasoning: Turn 18 exceeds 50 words and bundles multiple pieces of information (callback offer, credit warning, offer reminder, instruction to save number) into a single voice turn that is difficult to process; Turn 4 approaches the limit as well.

E1.evidence: Customer never explicitly stated financial hardship, emotional distress, or a specific difficulty beyond communication confusion; Turns 5-8 show ambiguous/garbled communication rather than stated distress; agent in Turn 7 attempts to clarify rather than show empathy.
E1.reasoning: The borrower never explicitly expressed financial hardship, distress, or a specific personal difficulty during this call; the communication challenges were more about audio/clarity issues than emotional distress requiring acknowledgment.

E2.evidence: DPD is 180 (high, NPA territory); customer signaled willingness to share in Turns 10-11 but never actually committed; Turn 18 uses mild credit impact language once; overall the agent applied almost zero meaningful pressure or urgency during the call.
E2.reasoning: With DPD at 180, applying credit consequence language is appropriate, but the agent effectively applied near-zero urgency throughout the call — no mention of NPA status, no escalating pressure, minimal engagement — representing under-pressure for a high-DPD stalling situation.

E3.evidence: Turn 12: Agent fires a stall response immediately after Turns 10-11 where customer said 'I can share. Not a problem.' without engaging; Turn 9: end_call function was fired while customer had said 'I understand. Would you be comfortable sharing...' and customer response 'I can share' was incoming; Turn 17: Customer says 'I'm waiting' — agent delivers closing speech suggesting it did not process this statement.
E3.reasoning: The end_call function was fired after Turn 9 while the customer was actively in a cooperative mode (having just said 'I can share'), and the agent's Turn 18 response to 'I'm waiting' demonstrates it did not process the customer's impatience or intent to share information.

E4.evidence: Turn 13: Customer pushes back somewhat confrontationally ('No. You asked me a question. Right? Why are you pulling out my account details now?') — agent responds in Turn 14 with 'I understand you're wondering... My apologies if that wasn't clear' — professional and apologetic; no sarcasm or aggression throughout.
E4.reasoning: When the customer pushed back in Turn 13, the agent responded professionally and apologetically; the tone remained respectful throughout the call with no matching of frustration or condescension.

O1.evidence: Turn 18: 'Would it be alright if I checked in with you in about a week?' — vague, no specific date; no amount confirmed by borrower; no PTP; no schedule_callback function called; call ends with 'feel free to call whenever you're ready' — no concrete commitment from borrower.
O1.reasoning: The call ended with no specific actionable next step: no PTP was reached, no callback was scheduled via function, no specific date or amount was confirmed by the borrower, and the closing language was vague ('in about a week', 'whenever you're ready').

O2.evidence: end_call reason: 'resolved_needs_time' — but borrower never said they needed time; borrower said 'I can share. Not a problem.' and 'I'm waiting'; proceed_to_closing called after Turn 8 ('I can't say') with commitment detail fabricated; no schedule_callback called despite Turn 18 promising a callback.
O2.reasoning: The end_call reason 'resolved_needs_time' is inaccurate — the borrower never requested time and was actively waiting to share information; the proceed_to_closing was premature and its commitment detail was fabricated; a callback was promised in dialogue but schedule_callback was never called.

O3.evidence: Customer arrived cooperative (Turns 10-11: 'I can share. Not a problem.') and willing to engage; agent stalled twice (Turns 12, 16), gave a wrong amount (Turn 4), ended the call prematurely (after Turn 9), and left the customer with no actionable information or scheduled follow-up; customer's confusion in Turn 13 suggests growing frustration.
O3.reasoning: A cooperative borrower who explicitly offered to share information was left worse off: they received a wrong rupee amount, were met with stalling behavior, and the call ended without any resolution, concrete next step, or even an accurate figure they could act upon.

worst_messages.agent_message: Okay, give me just a moment... Okay, Ravi Gupta, to help me understand better, could you tell me a little bit about what's making it difficult to pay the fifty-five thousand rupees for your DemoLender loan right now?
worst_messages.issue: The agent quotes 'fifty-five thousand rupees' — a figure that appears nowhere in the customer data (TOS is fifty thousand, POS is zero, settlement is blank). This is a fabricated number delivered authoritatively to the borrower. Compounding this, the agent re-asks the exact same discovery question already posed in Turn 3 before receiving any substantive answer, and does so without acknowledgment of the borrower's 'Yes' in Turn 2.
worst_messages.should_have_done: Since the borrower said 'Yes' in Turn 2 confirming identity, the agent should have disclosed the correct amounts per the Opening phase script: 'Great, Ravi. Your total outstanding is fifty thousand rupees, but we can remove all charges and close your loan at a lower amount. I wanted to understand what's been making it difficult to pay — what's going on on your end?' No invented figure, no repeated question.
worst_messages.impact: Introduced a false rupee amount (fifty-five thousand) that contradicts the actual TOS (fifty thousand), potentially confusing or misleading the borrower about what they owe. This undermines trust for the rest of the call and constitutes a material compliance risk. Repeating the unanswered question also wasted the only genuine engagement window before the conversation deteriorated.

worst_messages.agent_message: Let me pull up your account details...
worst_messages.issue: Immediately after the borrower signaled clear willingness to engage ('I can share. Not a problem.' in Turns 10-11), the agent delivered an empty stalling phrase that conveyed zero information and was never followed through. The customer had just removed their earlier hesitation and was actively waiting for a question — the agent responded with a meaningless placeholder. The customer rightly called this out in Turn 13.
worst_messages.should_have_done: The agent should have immediately capitalized on the cooperative signal with a direct, specific open question: 'That's great, Ravi. So what's the main thing that's been holding back the payment — is it a job situation, or something else going on?' No stalling, no filler — just a clear question while the borrower was ready to talk.
worst_messages.impact: Squandered the single best engagement opportunity in the entire call. The customer's willingness to share, which had been hard-won through several ambiguous turns, was met with an empty stall. This frustrated the customer (Turn 13: 'Why are you pulling out my account details now?'), eroded trust, and disrupted the conversational momentum. The call never recovered substantively after this point.

worst_messages.agent_message: Okay, I understand you need some time to consider the options. Would it be alright if I checked in with you in about a week? Also, just a reminder that every month the loan remains open, it adds a negative entry to your credit report. The offer for penalty removal will still be open when we next speak. Please save my number and feel free to call whenever you're ready.
worst_messages.issue: The customer had just said 'I'm waiting' — a direct signal of impatience and readiness to share information — yet the agent responded with a multi-part closing speech. The agent fabricated a 'needs time' framing the borrower never expressed, bundled four separate messages into one overlong voice turn, promised a callback without invoking schedule_callback, told the borrower to 'save my number and call whenever you're ready' (an unauthorized capability not supported by the system), and exited the call with no concrete commitment from the borrower.
worst_messages.should_have_done: The agent should have responded directly to 'I'm waiting' by finally asking the question the customer had been prepared to answer since Turn 11: 'Sorry for the delay — you mentioned you could share what's been going on. What's the main issue on your end right now?' If after that a callback was genuinely needed, it should have been scheduled via the schedule_callback function with a specific confirmed date and time, not a vague 'about a week.'
worst_messages.impact: Ended the call with no useful information gathered, no PTP, no scheduled callback function call, and a fabricated 'resolved_needs_time' disposition. A cooperative borrower who had actively waited to share their situation was dismissed with a generic script. The outcome was worse than if the call had never happened: the borrower received an incorrect rupee amount, experienced two stalls, and was told to call back on a number the agent cannot actually receive calls on.
